ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biscuit class; no plum-specific biscuit subclass exists. Mapped to nearest parent class FOODON_03301710.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 GR0AM covers biscuits and cookies broadly; no plum-specific variant code exists. Nearest defensible parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for plum biscuit; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of plum biscuit is approximately 3 biscuits (30 g), providing around 140 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Plum Biscuit — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Plum Biscuit (cr_id F11080046). Saturated fat estimated at approximately 7.5 g/100 g based on typical palm oil/shortening composition for this biscuit category, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this is documented as an approximation.

gi_source.title: Biscuits and cookies — GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database; plain sweet biscuit analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for plum biscuit found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 is estimated from the closest analogous food: plain sweet biscuit / shortbread-type biscuit (GI ~70, high category) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 21.0) / 100 = 14.7. Serving size used: 30 g (21.0 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 170 mg/100 g combined with high saturated fat (~7.5 g/100 g) from palm oil or shortening
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit plum biscuit intake; sodium at 170 mg/100 g is moderate but saturated fat from palm oil or shortening (~7.5 g/100 g) contributes to arterial stiffness, and the high refined carbohydrate load (70 g/100 g, zero fibre) promotes insulin-driven sodium retention — restrict to an occasional small portion and monitor overall daily sodium and saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high refined carbohydrate (70 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre, producing a high glycaemic index (~70) and high glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plum biscuit is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high refined carbohydrate content (70 g/100 g), zero dietary fibre, and high GI (~70), which will cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; if consumed, limit strictly to 1–2 biscuits and pair with a protein or fat source to partially blunt the glycaemic spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~7.5 g/100 g) from palm oil or shortening, zero dietary fibre, and high refined carbohydrate density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plum biscuit poses a high cardiovascular concern due to its estimated saturated fat content of approximately 7.5 g/100 g (from palm oil or vegetable shortening), zero dietary fibre, and high refined carbohydrate density (70 g/100 g), all of which adversely affect LDL cholesterol, triglycerides, and endothelial function; individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption and choose whole-grain, high-fibre alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Plum biscuit is a sweet, baked snack biscuit made from refined wheat flour, vegetable shortening or palm oil, sugar, and plum jam or plum flavouring. It is a commercially produced, ultra-processed product common in Singapore and Southeast Asian snack markets. The texture is typically crisp and crumbly, with a mildly sweet, fruity flavour from the plum component. Nutritionally, plum biscuit is energy-dense at 466 kcal per 100 g, with a high refined carbohydrate content of 70 g/100 g, 18 g fat/100 g (predominantly saturated fat from palm oil or shortening, estimated ~7.5 g/100 g), and 6 g protein/100 g. Dietary fibre is negligible at 0 g/100 g, and sugar is reported as 0 g/100 g by the HPB source, though the product contains plum jam or flavouring. Sodium is 170 mg/100 g. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 70 (high category), producing a glycaemic load of approximately 14.7 per 30 g serving. Clinically, plum biscuit is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high refined carbohydrate density, high saturated fat content, and zero dietary fibre. It is best consumed occasionally and in small portions as a discretionary snack.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a plum biscuit?
Is plum biscuit high in sugar?
What is the GI of plum biscuit?
Is plum biscuit suitable for hypertension?
How much fat is in a plum biscuit?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11080046, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat is approximated at ~7.5 g/100 g based on typical palm oil/shortening composition for this biscuit category and is documented as an estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest equivalent sweet biscuit class.
notes: Sugar_g is reported as 0.0 g/100 g per the HPB source — confirmed zero per approved source; the product contains plum jam or flavouring but sugar may be captured within the carbohydrate figure or may reflect a formulation using plum flavouring rather than jam. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 g/100 g — confirmed zero per HPB source for this refined biscuit product. Calcium_mg is 0.0 and iron_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific plum biscuit entry are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026, and no supplementary approved source (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy) provides an exact match for this product: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Saturated_fat_g of 7.5 g/100 g is an approximation based on typical palm oil/shortening composition for biscuits in this category and is not directly reported by HPB. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest equivalent plain sweet biscuit class; no direct GI measurement for plum biscuit was found. This is a generic food record (not a branded product); halal and kosher status are not confirmed and are set to false pending verification.
